{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = [[Ina, Nagano]] and [[Chino, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | range = [[Akaishi Mountains]] (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = [[Japan National Route 152]] }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between [[Ina City]] and [[Chino City]] in [[Nagano Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the [[Akaishi Mountains]] (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by [[Japan National Route 152]]. It is listed among the [[One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes]].
== Geography == Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the [[Suwa Basin]], the [[Yatsugatake Mountains]], [[Kiri-ga-mine]], and the distant [[Hida Mountains]] (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the [[Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line]]<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref>. To the southwest of the pass, [[Mount Moriya]], considered sacred to [[Suwa Taisha Shrine]], dominates the landscape<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>.
== History == The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late [[Taishō period]], a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving<ref name="nipponica" />.
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the [[Tōsandō]] route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the [[Sengoku period]], it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the [[Tenbun era]] (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage<ref name="kadokawa" />.
In the [[Edo period]], the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the [[Kai Province]] to Ina. In [[1933]], a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>.
== Tourism == At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over the [[Suwa Lake]] and the Northern Alps<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>.
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access == ; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from [[Chino Station]] on the [[JR Chūō Main Line]], taking about 30 minutes<ref name="tabinet" />. Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area. ; By Car From the [[Chūō Expressway]], the pass is about 15 kilometers from the [[Suwa Interchange]], with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture == Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''[[Laid-Back Camp]]'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by [[Afro (artist)|Afro]]. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website]

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = [[Ina, Nagano]] and [[Chino, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | range = [[Akaishi Mountains]] (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = [[Japan National Route 152]] }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between [[Ina City]] and [[Chino City]] in [[Nagano Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the [[Akaishi Mountains]] (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by [[Japan National Route 152]]. It is listed among the [[One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes]].
== Geography == Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the [[Suwa Basin]], the [[Yatsugatake Mountains]], [[Kiri-ga-mine]], and the distant [[Hida Mountains]] (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the [[Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line]]<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref>. To the southwest of the pass, [[Mount Moriya]], considered sacred to [[Suwa Taisha Shrine]], dominates the landscape<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>.
== History == The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late [[Taishō period]], a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving<ref name="nipponica" />.
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the [[Tōsandō]] route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the [[Sengoku period]], it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the [[Tenbun era]] (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage<ref name="kadokawa" />.
In the [[Edo period]], the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the [[Kai Province]] to Ina. In [[1933]], a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>.
== Tourism == At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over the [[Suwa Lake]] and the Northern Alps<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>.
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access == ; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from [[Chino Station]] on the [[JR Chūō Main Line]], taking about 30 minutes<ref name="tabinet" />. Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area. ; By Car From the [[Chūō Expressway]], the pass is about 15 kilometers from the [[Suwa Interchange]], with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture == Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''[[Laid-Back Camp]]'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by [[Afro (artist)|Afro]]. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>

The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />

Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />

; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

revision.text:
{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

revision.text:
{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website]

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website]

Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).

In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).

Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>

At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>

It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website]

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website]

'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website]

; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
