{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website]

The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).

In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

revision.text:
{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website]
==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖|12=draft}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線|12=draft}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道|12=draft}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|12=draft}}, with parking available near the summit.

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市|12=draft}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈|12=draft}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号|12=draft}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|12=draft}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈|12=draft}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号|12=draft}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠|12=draft}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰|12=draft}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈|12=draft}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線|12=draft}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山|12=draft}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=draft}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道|12=draft}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=draft}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国|12=draft}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖|12=draft}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線|12=draft}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道|12=draft}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|12=draft}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△|12=draft}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|12=draft}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖|12=draft}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線|12=draft}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道|12=draft}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|12=draft}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△|12=draft}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|12=draft}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市|12=draft}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈|12=draft}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号|12=draft}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|12=draft}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈|12=draft}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号|12=draft}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠|12=draft}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰|12=draft}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈|12=draft}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線|12=draft}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山|12=draft}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=draft}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道|12=draft}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=draft}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国|12=draft}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖|12=draft}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線|12=draft}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道|12=draft}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|12=draft}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△|12=draft}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|12=draft}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox 峠 |名称 = 杖突峠 |画像 = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|伊那方面（2010年）]] |画像キャプション = 伊那方面（2010年） |標高 = 1,247『角川日本地名大辞典 20 長野県』730ページ『日本歴史地名大系 20 長野県の地名』408ページ。 |緯度度 = 35 |緯度分 = 58 |緯度秒 = 17.8 |経度度 =138 |経度分 = 07 |経度秒 = 28.1 |所在地 = [[長野県]][[伊那市]]・[[茅野市]]座標は[http://wikitools.web.fc2.com/geo/ ジオロケーター 日本語版]にて伊那市と茅野市との境界線と国道152号との交点にマーカーをドラッグして得た（2018年2月26日閲覧）。 |山系 = [[赤石山脈]]{{Cite web|和書|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%9D%96%E7%AA%81%E5%B3%A0-99010#E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E5.85.A8.E6.9B.B8.28.E3.83.8B.E3.83.83.E3.83.9D.E3.83.8B.E3.82.AB.29 |title=コトバンク 杖突峠とは 日本大百科全書（ニッポニカ）の解説 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}（南アルプス） |交通路 = [[国道152号]] }} {{Ja Route Sign|152|align=left}} '''杖突峠'''（つえつきとうげ）は、[[長野県]][[伊那市]]と[[茅野市]]との境にある[[峠]]。標高1,247[[メートル]]。古くは[[東山道]]、[[杖突街道]]、[[国道256号]]が通り、現在は[[国道152号]]が通っている{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mapion.co.jp/m2/35.97168525938968,138.12445161804104,15 |title=長野県茅野市宮川の地図 |publisher=[[Mapion]] |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}。[[日本百名峠]]。 == 地理 == [[伊那市]][[高遠町]]藤澤と[[茅野市]]宮川との境に位置する。[[赤石山脈]]（南アルプス）の北端にあたり、峠一帯を「晴ヶ峰」という。伊那側は藤沢川が流れる[[谷]]に沿った道で、比較的傾斜が緩い。一方、茅野側は[[糸魚川静岡構造線]]が通過している関係で[[断層崖]]となっており、急坂である。峠から[[西南西]]の方角には[[諏訪大社]]の[[神体]]とされる[[守屋山]]がそびえる{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.cbr.mlit.go.jp/tenjyo/think/heritage/pdf/087.pdf |title=人と暮らしの伊那谷遺産プロジェクト 杖突峠 |publisher=[[国土交通省]]天竜川上流河川事務所 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}。 [[File:OSM Tsuetsuki Pass.png|thumb|left|360px|杖突峠周辺の地図（[[オープンストリートマップ]]）
峠を通過する国道152号は、頂上を境にして茅野側（右上）は蛇行、伊那側（左下）は直線と、全く異なる線形をしている。]] {{Clearleft}} == 歴史 == むかし、人々がこの急坂を[[杖]]を突きながら登ったことが「杖突峠」の名の起こりとされ、頂上で捨てられた杖を燃やして[[供養]]するという行事が[[大正]]末期まで行われていた。異説として、[[儀式]]によって[[地上]]に降臨した[[神]]が杖を突く場所であったからとも言われている。 [[古代]]より[[東山道]]が通過し、[[伊那地域]]（[[伊那郡]]）と[[諏訪地域]]（[[諏訪郡]]）とを結ぶ主要な[[道路]]である。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]には軍事上の要所とされ、[[天文 (元号)|天文]]11年の[[武田信玄]]と[[諏訪頼重 (戦国時代)|諏訪頼重]]との戦いでは、信玄に呼応した[[高遠頼継]]が伊那から峠を越えて諏訪へと攻め込んだ。天文年間の[[古文書]]に「つへつきたうけ」・「杖ツキ峠」といった記載が見える。軍事面以外にも、[[秋葉山本宮秋葉神社]]（[[遠江国]]、現・[[静岡県]]）への参詣路としての役割もあった。 [[江戸時代]]になると峠道が「杖突[[街道]]」もしくは「藤沢街道」と呼ばれるようになり、[[中馬]]の往来が盛んに行われた。[[塩]]や[[魚]]、[[茶]]といった[[甲斐国|甲州]]（現・[[山梨県]]）方面からの物資がこの街道を通じて伊那へと運ばれた{{Cite web|和書|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%9D%96%E7%AA%81%E5%B3%A0-99010#E4.B8.96.E7.95.8C.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E4.BA.8B.E5.85.B8.20.E7.AC.AC.EF.BC.92.E7.89.88 |title=コトバンク 杖突峠とは 世界大百科事典 第2版の解説 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}。[[八ヶ岳]]の裾野に[[用水路]]を開いた[[坂本市之丞|坂本養川]]は、人々に[[提灯]]を持たせて各所に配置し、杖突峠からその明かりを目印に[[測量]]を行ったとの[[伝説]]がある{{Cite sign |title=坂本養川のしごと |type=案内板 |location=[[茅野市尖石縄文考古館]] 屋外「坂本養川翁」像隣 |date=2012-05-20 }}。 [[近代]]に入り街道は一旦衰退を見せるものの、[[1933年]]（[[昭和]]8年）になって[[車道]]が開通している。 == 観光 == 頂上に「峠の[[茶屋]]」（茅野市宮川3673）があり、[[展望台]]が設置されている{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-tabi.net/modules/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html |title=杖突峠/峠の茶屋 無料展望台 |publisher=長野県観光機構 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}。[[諏訪盆地]]が一望でき、[[八ヶ岳連峰]]に[[霧ヶ峰]]、遠く[[飛騨山脈]]（北アルプス）まで見渡すことができる。[[夕|夕方]]になると飛騨山脈に沈む[[太陽]]からの[[夕焼け|夕日]]が差し、赤く照らされた[[諏訪湖]]や[[八ヶ岳]]が美しい。 峠の茶屋は[[1984年]]（昭和59年）に建設されたもので、[[建物]]は[[鉄骨構造]]の2階建て。延床面積は466[[平方メートル]]である。[[2001年]]（[[平成]]13年）[[12月]]からは茅野市と伊那市（当時・[[上伊那郡]][[高遠町]]）との共同所有となり、[[2017年]]（平成29年）3月、茅野市・伊那市の両市は[[2012年]]（平成24年）4月から[[テナント]]として入居していた伸和コントロールズ（[[神奈川県]][[川崎市]]）に施設を売却。1階部分を[[多目的ホール]]（面積約80平方メートル）に改装し、同年[[7月]]にオープンした{{Cite news |url=http://www.nagano-np.co.jp/articles/14153 |title=峠の茶屋 伸和コントロールズに売却 |newspaper=[[長野日報|Nagano Nippo Web]] |publisher=長野日報社 |date=2017-03-04 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}{{Cite news |url=http://www.nagano-np.co.jp/articles/19760 |title=諏訪湖と八ケ岳一望 峠の茶屋多目的ホール |newspaper=Nagano Nippo Web |publisher=長野日報社 |date=2017-07-22 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}。 {{wide image|Suwa Basin view from Tsuetsuki Pass 2012-05.jpg|1196px|{{Commonscat-inline|Views from Tsuetsuki Pass|杖突峠からの眺望}}|alt=展望台より望む}} == 交通アクセス == ;[[公共交通機関]] :[[東日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[中央本線]]・[[茅野駅]]から[[タクシー]]で30[[分]]間。 :峠に「[[守屋山|守屋]]登山口」[[バス停留所|バス停]]があり、主に[[春]]（[[4月]]）限定で[[ジェイアールバス関東]]が茅野駅から当バス停を経て高遠方面への[[路線バス]]を運行している{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jrbuskanto.co.jp/bus_etc/cntimep01.cfm?pa=1&pb=1&pc=j0480121&pd=0&st=1 |title=一般路線バスのご案内 【平日】高遠～四日市場～伊那藤沢～古屋敷～茅野駅 |publisher=ジェイアールバス関東 |date=2015-03-14 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jrbuskanto.co.jp/bus_etc/cntimep01.cfm?pa=1&pb=1&pc=j0480123&pd=0&st=1 |title=一般路線バスのご案内 【土休日】高遠～四日市場～伊那藤沢～古屋敷～茅野駅 |publisher=ジェイアールバス関東 |date=2015-03-14 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}。 ;[[自家用自動車]] :[[中央自動車道]]・[[諏訪インターチェンジ]]から15[[キロメートル]]、[[自動車]]で30分間。普通車20台が駐車可能な[[駐車場]]がある。 File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|茅野方面（峠の茶屋） File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|パノラマ展望台 File:R152-264.jpg|[[国道152号]]沿いの
杖突峠案内標識
長野県茅野市 == その他 == * 茅野側の峠道沿いの私有地内に、奇妙な[[スフィンクス]]の像が置かれている。[[ごみ]]の[[不法投棄]]に頭を悩ませていた地元住民が設置したもので、像を設置して以降、不法投棄はなくなったという{{Cite news |url=http://fundo.jp/98162 |title=顔はツタンカーメン、体はスフィンクス…不法投棄をピタッと止めた置き物が話題に！ |newspaper=FUNDO |publisher=リプルゼ |date=2016-10-05 |accessdate=2018-03-03 }}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.google.co.jp/maps/@35.9803631,138.1425298,3a,15y,261.16h,85.98t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1snivymhYAC_s0_slM66R2YQ!2e0!7i13312!8i6656?hl=ja&shorturl=1 |title=Google ストリートビュー 国道152号線 茅野市, 長野県 |publisher=[[Google]] |date=2015-06 |accessdate=2018-03-03 }}。 * [[あfろ]]の[[漫画]]『[[ゆるキャン△]]』第16話（[[単行本]]第3巻収録あfろ『ゆるキャン△ 3』[[芳文社]]、2017年。ISBN 4832248049、[[テレビアニメ]]第9話）では、山梨県から長野県[[上伊那地域]]に向かう主人公が立ち寄った場所として登場。展望台からの景色を[[スマートフォン]]で撮影し、[[写真]]を病床の友人に贈った。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会・[[竹内理三]]編『[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 20 長野県』[[角川書店]]、1990年7月18日。ISBN 4040012003 * 『[[日本歴史地名大系]] 20 長野県の地名』[[平凡社]]、1979年11月25日。ISBN 4582490204 == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat}} * [[日本の峠一覧]] - [[日本百名峠]] * [[国道152号]] * [[中央構造線]] - [[板山露頭]] - [[南アルプス（中央構造線エリア）ジオパーク]] * [[守屋山]] * [[伊那谷]] * [[高遠合戦]] * [[高遠電気軌道]] * [[杖衝坂]]（[[三重県]]） == 外部リンク == * {{Kotobank|2=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典ほか}} * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/modules/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html 長野県公式観光ウェブサイト さわやか信州旅.net 杖突峠/峠の茶屋 無料展望台] * [http://live.lcv.ne.jp/cgi-bin/road/movie.php?camera_id=8 杖突峠ライブカメラ（PC以外は登録会員のみ）] - [[エルシーブイ]] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ 杖突峠 峠の茶屋] {{デフォルトソート:つえつきとうけ}} [[Category:長野県の峠]] [[Category:伊那市の交通]] [[Category:伊那市の地理]] [[Category:茅野市の交通]] [[Category:茅野市の地理]] [[Category:国道152号]] [[Category:南アルプス]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[杖突峠]] to [[Tsuetzuki Pass]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{Infobox 峠 |名称 = 杖突峠 |画像 = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|伊那方面（2010年）]] |画像キャプション = 伊那方面（2010年） |標高 = 1,247『角川日本地名大辞典 20 長野県』730ページ『日本歴史地名大系 20 長野県の地名』408ページ。 |緯度度 = 35 |緯度分 = 58 |緯度秒 = 17.8 |経度度 =138 |経度分 = 07 |経度秒 = 28.1 |所在地 = [[長野県]][[伊那市]]・[[茅野市]]座標は[http://wikitools.web.fc2.com/geo/ ジオロケーター 日本語版]にて伊那市と茅野市との境界線と国道152号との交点にマーカーをドラッグして得た（2018年2月26日閲覧）。 |山系 = [[赤石山脈]]{{Cite web|和書|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%9D%96%E7%AA%81%E5%B3%A0-99010#E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E5.85.A8.E6.9B.B8.28.E3.83.8B.E3.83.83.E3.83.9D.E3.83.8B.E3.82.AB.29 |title=コトバンク 杖突峠とは 日本大百科全書（ニッポニカ）の解説 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}（南アルプス） |交通路 = [[国道152号]] }} {{Ja Route Sign|152|align=left}} '''杖突峠'''（つえつきとうげ）は、[[長野県]][[伊那市]]と[[茅野市]]との境にある[[峠]]。標高1,247[[メートル]]。古くは[[東山道]]、[[杖突街道]]、[[国道256号]]が通り、現在は[[国道152号]]が通っている{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mapion.co.jp/m2/35.97168525938968,138.12445161804104,15 |title=長野県茅野市宮川の地図 |publisher=[[Mapion]] |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}。[[日本百名峠]]。 == 地理 == [[伊那市]][[高遠町]]藤澤と[[茅野市]]宮川との境に位置する。[[赤石山脈]]（南アルプス）の北端にあたり、峠一帯を「晴ヶ峰」という。伊那側は藤沢川が流れる[[谷]]に沿った道で、比較的傾斜が緩い。一方、茅野側は[[糸魚川静岡構造線]]が通過している関係で[[断層崖]]となっており、急坂である。峠から[[西南西]]の方角には[[諏訪大社]]の[[神体]]とされる[[守屋山]]がそびえる{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.cbr.mlit.go.jp/tenjyo/think/heritage/pdf/087.pdf |title=人と暮らしの伊那谷遺産プロジェクト 杖突峠 |publisher=[[国土交通省]]天竜川上流河川事務所 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}。 [[File:OSM Tsuetsuki Pass.png|thumb|left|360px|杖突峠周辺の地図（[[オープンストリートマップ]]）
峠を通過する国道152号は、頂上を境にして茅野側（右上）は蛇行、伊那側（左下）は直線と、全く異なる線形をしている。]] {{Clearleft}} == 歴史 == むかし、人々がこの急坂を[[杖]]を突きながら登ったことが「杖突峠」の名の起こりとされ、頂上で捨てられた杖を燃やして[[供養]]するという行事が[[大正]]末期まで行われていた。異説として、[[儀式]]によって[[地上]]に降臨した[[神]]が杖を突く場所であったからとも言われている。 [[古代]]より[[東山道]]が通過し、[[伊那地域]]（[[伊那郡]]）と[[諏訪地域]]（[[諏訪郡]]）とを結ぶ主要な[[道路]]である。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]には軍事上の要所とされ、[[天文 (元号)|天文]]11年の[[武田信玄]]と[[諏訪頼重 (戦国時代)|諏訪頼重]]との戦いでは、信玄に呼応した[[高遠頼継]]が伊那から峠を越えて諏訪へと攻め込んだ。天文年間の[[古文書]]に「つへつきたうけ」・「杖ツキ峠」といった記載が見える。軍事面以外にも、[[秋葉山本宮秋葉神社]]（[[遠江国]]、現・[[静岡県]]）への参詣路としての役割もあった。 [[江戸時代]]になると峠道が「杖突[[街道]]」もしくは「藤沢街道」と呼ばれるようになり、[[中馬]]の往来が盛んに行われた。[[塩]]や[[魚]]、[[茶]]といった[[甲斐国|甲州]]（現・[[山梨県]]）方面からの物資がこの街道を通じて伊那へと運ばれた{{Cite web|和書|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%9D%96%E7%AA%81%E5%B3%A0-99010#E4.B8.96.E7.95.8C.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E4.BA.8B.E5.85.B8.20.E7.AC.AC.EF.BC.92.E7.89.88 |title=コトバンク 杖突峠とは 世界大百科事典 第2版の解説 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}。[[八ヶ岳]]の裾野に[[用水路]]を開いた[[坂本市之丞|坂本養川]]は、人々に[[提灯]]を持たせて各所に配置し、杖突峠からその明かりを目印に[[測量]]を行ったとの[[伝説]]がある{{Cite sign |title=坂本養川のしごと |type=案内板 |location=[[茅野市尖石縄文考古館]] 屋外「坂本養川翁」像隣 |date=2012-05-20 }}。 [[近代]]に入り街道は一旦衰退を見せるものの、[[1933年]]（[[昭和]]8年）になって[[車道]]が開通している。 == 観光 == 頂上に「峠の[[茶屋]]」（茅野市宮川3673）があり、[[展望台]]が設置されている{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-tabi.net/modules/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html |title=杖突峠/峠の茶屋 無料展望台 |publisher=長野県観光機構 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}。[[諏訪盆地]]が一望でき、[[八ヶ岳連峰]]に[[霧ヶ峰]]、遠く[[飛騨山脈]]（北アルプス）まで見渡すことができる。[[夕|夕方]]になると飛騨山脈に沈む[[太陽]]からの[[夕焼け|夕日]]が差し、赤く照らされた[[諏訪湖]]や[[八ヶ岳]]が美しい。 news |url=http://www.nagano-np.co.jp/articles/14153 |title=峠の茶屋 伸和コントロールズに売却 |newspaper=[[長野日報|Nagano Nippo Web]] |publisher=長野日報社 |date=2017-03-04 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}{{Cite news |url=http://www.nagano-np.co.jp/articles/19760 |title=諏訪湖と八ケ岳一望 峠の茶屋多目的ホール |newspaper=Nagano Nippo Web |publisher=長野日報社 |date=2017-07-22 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}。 {{wide image|Suwa Basin view from Tsuetsuki Pass 2012-05.jpg|1196px|{{Commonscat-inline|Views from Tsuetsuki Pass|杖突峠からの眺望}}|alt=展望台より望む}} == 交通アクセス == ;[[公共交通機関]] :[[東日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[中央本線]]・[[茅野駅]]から[[タクシー]]で30[[分]]間。 :峠に「[[守屋山|守屋]]登山口」[[バス停留所|バス停]]があり、主に[[春]]（[[4月]]）限定で[[ジェイアールバス関東]]が茅野駅から当バス停を経て高遠方面への[[路線バス]]を運行している{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jrbuskanto.co.jp/bus_etc/cntimep01.cfm?pa=1&pb=1&pc=j0480121&pd=0&st=1 |title=一般路線バスのご案内 【平日】高遠～四日市場～伊那藤沢～古屋敷～茅野駅 |publisher=ジェイアールバス関東 |date=2015-03-14 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jrbuskanto.co.jp/bus_etc/cntimep01.cfm?pa=1&pb=1&pc=j0480123&pd=0&st=1 |title=一般路線バスのご案内 【土休日】高遠～四日市場～伊那藤沢～古屋敷～茅野駅 |publisher=ジェイアールバス関東 |date=2015-03-14 |accessdate=2018-02-26 }}。 ;[[自家用自動車]] :[[中央自動車道]]・[[諏訪インターチェンジ]]から15[[キロメートル]]、[[自動車]]で30分間。普通車20台が駐車可能な[[駐車場]]がある。 File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|茅野方面（峠の茶屋） File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|パノラマ展望台 File:R152-264.jpg|[[国道152号]]沿いの
杖突峠案内標識
長野県茅野市 == その他 == * 茅野側の峠道沿いの私有地内に、奇妙な[[スフィンクス]]の像が置かれている。[[ごみ]]の[[不法投棄]]に頭を悩ませていた地元住民が設置したもので、像を設置して以降、不法投棄はなくなったという{{Cite news |url=http://fundo.jp/98162 |title=顔はツタンカーメン、体はスフィンクス…不法投棄をピタッと止めた置き物が話題に！ |newspaper=FUNDO |publisher=リプルゼ |date=2016-10-05 |accessdate=2018-03-03 }}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.google.co.jp/maps/@35.9803631,138.1425298,3a,15y,261.16h,85.98t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1snivymhYAC_s0_slM66R2YQ!2e0!7i13312!8i6656?hl=ja&shorturl=1 |title=Google ストリートビュー 国道152号線 茅野市, 長野県 |publisher=[[Google]] |date=2015-06 |accessdate=2018-03-03 }}。 * [[あfろ]]の[[漫画]]『[[ゆるキャン△]]』第16話（[[単行本]]第3巻収録あfろ『ゆるキャン△ 3』[[芳文社]]、2017年。ISBN 4832248049、[[テレビアニメ]]第9話）では、山梨県から長野県[[上伊那地域]]に向かう主人公が立ち寄った場所として登場。展望台からの景色を[[スマートフォン]]で撮影し、[[写真]]を病床の友人に贈った。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会・[[竹内理三]]編『[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 20 長野県』[[角川書店]]、1990年7月18日。ISBN 4040012003 * 『[[日本歴史地名大系]] 20 長野県の地名』[[平凡社]]、1979年11月25日。ISBN 4582490204 == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat}} * [[日本の峠一覧]] - [[日本百名峠]] * [[国道152号]] * [[中央構造線]] - [[板山露頭]] - [[南アルプス（中央構造線エリア）ジオパーク]] * [[守屋山]] * [[伊那谷]] * [[高遠合戦]] * [[高遠電気軌道]] * [[杖衝坂]]（[[三重県]]） == 外部リンク == * {{Kotobank|2=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典ほか}} * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/modules/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html 長野県公式観光ウェブサイト さわやか信州旅.net 杖突峠/峠の茶屋 無料展望台] * [http://live.lcv.ne.jp/cgi-bin/road/movie.php?camera_id=8 杖突峠ライブカメラ（PC以外は登録会員のみ）] - [[エルシーブイ]] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ 杖突峠 峠の茶屋] {{デフォルトソート:つえつきとうけ}} [[Category:長野県の峠]] [[Category:伊那市の交通]] [[Category:伊那市の地理]] [[Category:茅野市の交通]] [[Category:茅野市の地理]] [[Category:国道152号]] [[Category:南アルプス]]

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号|qq=draft|qq=draft}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号|qq=draft|qq=draft}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

{{Infobox Mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina, Nagano|qq=}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|ja|茅野市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chino, Nagano|qq=}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaishi Mountains|qq=}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan National Route 152|qq=}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina City|qq=}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chino City|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|ja|赤石山脈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaishi Mountains|qq=}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|ja|国道152号|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan National Route 152|qq=}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|qq=}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Basin|qq=}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳連峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsugatake Mountains|qq=}}, {{ill|Kirigamine|ja|霧ヶ峰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirigamine|qq=}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|ja|飛騨山脈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hida Mountains|qq=}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|qq=}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Moriya|qq=}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa-taisha|qq=}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|qq=}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōsandō|qq=}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|qq=}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Province|qq=}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lake Suwa|qq=}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chino Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Chūō Main Line|qq=}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūō Expressway|qq=}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Interchange|qq=}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Laid-Back Camp|qq=}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afro (artist)|qq=}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|ja|糸魚川静岡構造線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|qq=}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Moriya|qq=}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa-taisha|qq=}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|qq=}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|ja|東山道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōsandō|qq=}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|qq=}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Province|qq=}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|ja|諏訪湖|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lake Suwa|qq=}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|ja|茅野駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chino Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|JR Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Chūō Main Line|qq=}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|ja|中央自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūō Expressway|qq=}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Interchange|qq=}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|ja|ゆるキャン△|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Laid-Back Camp|qq=}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afro (artist)|qq=}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina City|ja|伊那市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina City|qq=}} and {{ill|Chino City|ja|茅野市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chino City|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}}. <gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from [[:en:Chino Station|Chino Station]] on the [[:en:Chūō Main Line|JR Chūō Main Line]], taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the [[:en:Chūō Expressway|Chūō Expressway]], the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Interchange|qq=}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==

In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

revision.text:
{{Infobox mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]] and [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | range = [[:en:Akaishi Mountains|Akaishi Mountains]] (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = [[:en:Japan National Route 152|Japan National Route 152]] }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina City]] and [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino City]] in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the [[:en:Akaishi Mountains|Akaishi Mountains]] (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by [[:en:Japan National Route 152|Japan National Route 152]]. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|qq=}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Basin|qq=}}, the [[:en:Yatsugatake Mountains|Yatsugatake Mountains]], [[:en:Mount Kirigamine|Kirigamine]], and the distant [[:en:Hida Mountains|Hida Mountains]] (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the [[:en:Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line]].<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Moriya|qq=}}, considered sacred to [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|qq=}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the [[:en:Tōsandō|Tōsandō]] route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|qq=}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]] to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over [[:en:Lake Suwa|Lake Suwa]] and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from [[:en:Chino Station|Chino Station]] on the [[:en:Chūō Main Line|JR Chūō Main Line]], taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the [[:en:Chūō Expressway|Chūō Expressway]], the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Interchange|qq=}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''[[:en:Laid-Back Camp|Laid-Back Camp]]'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afro (artist)|qq=}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]] to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over [[:en:Lake Suwa|Lake Suwa]] and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from [[:en:Chino Station|Chino Station]] on the [[:en:Chūō Main Line|JR Chūō Main Line]], taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the [[:en:Chūō Expressway|Chūō Expressway]], the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Interchange|qq=}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''[[:en:Laid-Back Camp|Laid-Back Camp]]'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afro (artist)|qq=}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
