<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsuetsuki Pass]][[ja:杖突峠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsuetsuki Pass]][[ja:杖突峠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsuetsuki Pass]][[ja:杖突峠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11523572]] Wikidata name: Tsuetsuki Pass [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina, Nagano}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|WD=Q838660|lt=Chino, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|en|Akaishi Mountains|WD=Q415350|lt=Akaishi Mountains}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|en|Japan National Route 152|WD=Q6156814|lt=Japan National Route 152}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina City}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|WD=Q838660|lt=Chino City}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|en|Akaishi Mountains|WD=Q415350|lt=Akaishi Mountains}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|en|Japan National Route 152|WD=Q6156814|lt=Japan National Route 152}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|1=One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21015501}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Basin|1=Suwa Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631910}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|en|Yatsugatake Mountains|WD=Q11390433|lt=Yatsugatake Mountains}}, {{ill|Mount Kirigamine|en|Mount Kirigamine|WD=Q3695499|lt=Kirigamine}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|en|Hida Mountains|WD=Q1072070|lt=Hida Mountains}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|en|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|WD=Q906591|lt=Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Moriya|qq=}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|en|Tōsandō|WD=Q1334011|lt=Tōsandō}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|en|Lake Suwa|WD=Q1206692|lt=Lake Suwa}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|en|Chino Station|WD=Q844063|lt=Chino Station}} on the {{ill|Chūō Main Line|en|Chūō Main Line|WD=Q1078110|lt=JR Chūō Main Line}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|en|Chūō Expressway|WD=Q1091276|lt=Chūō Expressway}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Interchange|1=Suwa interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631823}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|en|Laid-Back Camp|WD=Q28691353|lt=Laid-Back Camp}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afro (artist)|1=Afro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q47465052}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsuetsuki Pass]][[ja:杖突峠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsuetsuki Pass]][[ja:杖突峠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsuetsuki Pass]][[ja:杖突峠]]
{{Infobox mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina, Nagano|1=Ina, Nagano}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|WD=Q838660|lt=Chino, Nagano|1=Chino, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|en|Akaishi Mountains|WD=Q415350|lt=Akaishi Mountains|1=Akaishi Mountains}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|en|Japan National Route 152|WD=Q6156814|lt=Japan National Route 152|1=Japan National Route 152}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina City|1=Ina, Nagano}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|WD=Q838660|lt=Chino City|1=Chino, Nagano}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|en|Akaishi Mountains|WD=Q415350|lt=Akaishi Mountains|1=Akaishi Mountains}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|en|Japan National Route 152|WD=Q6156814|lt=Japan National Route 152|1=Japan National Route 152}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|1=Q21015501|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21015501}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Basin|1=Suwa Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631910}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|en|Yatsugatake Mountains|WD=Q11390433|lt=Yatsugatake Mountains|1=Yatsugatake Mountains}}, {{ill|Mount Kirigamine|en|Mount Kirigamine|WD=Q3695499|lt=Kirigamine|1=Mount Kirigamine}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|en|Hida Mountains|WD=Q1072070|lt=Hida Mountains|1=Hida Mountains}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|en|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|WD=Q906591|lt=Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|1=Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Moriya|qq=}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|en|Tōsandō|WD=Q1334011|lt=Tōsandō|1=Tōsandō}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|1=Tenmon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province|1=Kai Province}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|en|Lake Suwa|WD=Q1206692|lt=Lake Suwa|1=Lake Suwa}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|en|Chino Station|WD=Q844063|lt=Chino Station|1=Chino Station}} on the {{ill|Chūō Main Line|en|Chūō Main Line|WD=Q1078110|lt=JR Chūō Main Line|1=Chūō Main Line}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|en|Chūō Expressway|WD=Q1091276|lt=Chūō Expressway|1=Chūō Expressway}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Interchange|1=Suwa interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631823}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|en|Laid-Back Camp|WD=Q28691353|lt=Laid-Back Camp|1=Laid-Back Camp}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afro (artist)|1=Afro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q47465052}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Kadokawa Shoten, 1990. * ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei,'' Vol. 20: Nagano. Heibonsha, 1979.
== External Links == * [http://www.nagano-tabi.net/enjoy/enjoy_14020002.html Nagano Prefecture Tourism Website - Tsuetsuki Pass] * [http://tsuetsuki.com/ Official Toge no Chaya Website] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

{{Infobox mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina, Nagano|1=Ina, Nagano}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|WD=Q838660|lt=Chino, Nagano|1=Chino, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|en|Akaishi Mountains|WD=Q415350|lt=Akaishi Mountains|1=Akaishi Mountains}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|en|Japan National Route 152|WD=Q6156814|lt=Japan National Route 152|1=Japan National Route 152}} }}

; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|en|Chino Station|WD=Q844063|lt=Chino Station|1=Chino Station}} on the {{ill|Chūō Main Line|en|Chūō Main Line|WD=Q1078110|lt=JR Chūō Main Line|1=Chūō Main Line}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|en|Chūō Expressway|WD=Q1091276|lt=Chūō Expressway|1=Chūō Expressway}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Interchange|1=Suwa interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631823}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|en|Laid-Back Camp|WD=Q28691353|lt=Laid-Back Camp|1=Laid-Back Camp}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afro (artist)|1=Afro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q47465052}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsuetsuki Pass]][[ja:杖突峠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsuetsuki Pass]][[ja:杖突峠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Tsuetsuki Pass]][[ja:杖突峠]]
{{Infobox mountain pass | name = Tsuetsuki Pass | photo = [[File:Tsuetsuki Pass 01.jpg|300px|View toward Ina (2010)]] | photo_caption = View toward Ina (2010) | elevation_m = 1247 | location = {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina, Nagano|1=Ina, Nagano}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|WD=Q838660|lt=Chino, Nagano|1=Chino, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | range = {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|en|Akaishi Mountains|WD=Q415350|lt=Akaishi Mountains|1=Akaishi Mountains}} (Southern Alps) | coordinates = {{Coord|35|58|17.8|N|138|07|28.1|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | traversed = {{ill|Japan National Route 152|en|Japan National Route 152|WD=Q6156814|lt=Japan National Route 152|1=Japan National Route 152}} }}
'''Tsuetsuki Pass''' (杖突峠, Tsuetsuki-tōge) is a mountain pass located on the border between {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina City|1=Ina, Nagano}} and {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|WD=Q838660|lt=Chino City|1=Chino, Nagano}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. At an elevation of 1,247 meters, it lies at the northern end of the {{ill|Akaishi Mountains|en|Akaishi Mountains|WD=Q415350|lt=Akaishi Mountains|1=Akaishi Mountains}} (Southern Alps) and serves as an important transportation route, currently traversed by {{ill|Japan National Route 152|en|Japan National Route 152|WD=Q6156814|lt=Japan National Route 152|1=Japan National Route 152}}. It is listed among the {{ill|One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|ja|日本百名峠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/One Hundred Famous Japanese Passes|1=Q21015501|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21015501}}.
== Geography ==
Tsuetsuki Pass is situated between Fujisawa in Ina City and Miyagawa in Chino City. The pass marks the northern end of the Akaishi Mountains, with the surrounding area known as "Harugamine" (晴ヶ峰). The pass offers panoramic views of the {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Basin|1=Suwa Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631910}}, the {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|en|Yatsugatake Mountains|WD=Q11390433|lt=Yatsugatake Mountains|1=Yatsugatake Mountains}}, {{ill|Mount Kirigamine|en|Mount Kirigamine|WD=Q3695499|lt=Kirigamine|1=Mount Kirigamine}}, and the distant {{ill|Hida Mountains|en|Hida Mountains|WD=Q1072070|lt=Hida Mountains|1=Hida Mountains}} (Northern Alps).
On the Ina side, the route follows a relatively gentle incline along the Fujisawa River valley. In contrast, the Chino side features a steep escarpment formed by the {{ill|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|en|Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|WD=Q906591|lt=Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line|1=Itoigawa-Shizuoka Tectonic Line}}.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten, Vol. 20: Nagano</ref> To the southwest of the pass, {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Moriya|qq=}}, considered sacred to {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}}, dominates the landscape.<ref name="nipponica">Nipponica Encyclopedia</ref>
== History ==
The name "Tsuetsuki" (杖突) is said to originate from travelers needing walking sticks (杖, tsue) to climb the steep slope. Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|en|Tōsandō|WD=Q1334011|lt=Tōsandō|1=Tōsandō}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|1=Tenmon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province|1=Kai Province}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|en|Lake Suwa|WD=Q1206692|lt=Lake Suwa|1=Lake Suwa}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|en|Chino Station|WD=Q844063|lt=Chino Station|1=Chino Station}} on the {{ill|Chūō Main Line|en|Chūō Main Line|WD=Q1078110|lt=JR Chūō Main Line|1=Chūō Main Line}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|en|Chūō Expressway|WD=Q1091276|lt=Chūō Expressway|1=Chūō Expressway}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Interchange|1=Suwa interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631823}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|en|Laid-Back Camp|WD=Q28691353|lt=Laid-Back Camp|1=Laid-Back Camp}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afro (artist)|1=Afro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q47465052}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.

Until the late {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q329174}}, a local tradition involved burning discarded walking sticks at the summit in a ritual of thanksgiving.<ref name="nipponica" />
Since ancient times, the pass was part of the {{ill|Tōsandō|en|Tōsandō|WD=Q1334011|lt=Tōsandō|1=Tōsandō}} route connecting the Ina and Suwa regions. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, it was a strategically important route. Historical records from the {{ill|Tenbun era|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun era|1=Tenmon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} (1532–1555) mention "Tsue-tsuki-tōge," indicating its significance as a military and commercial passage.<ref name="kadokawa" />
In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the pass was part of the "Tsuetsuki Kaidō" or "Fujisawa Kaidō," facilitating trade and transportation of goods such as salt, fish, and tea from the {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province|1=Kai Province}} to Ina. In 1933, a motorable road was constructed, revitalizing its role in local transportation.<ref name="sekai">Sekai Daihyakka Encyclopedia</ref>
== Tourism ==
At the summit, the "Toge no Chaya" (峠の茶屋), a rest area with a viewing platform, provides expansive views of the surrounding mountains and valleys, including stunning sunsets over {{ill|Lake Suwa|en|Lake Suwa|WD=Q1206692|lt=Lake Suwa|1=Lake Suwa}} and the Northern Alps.<ref name="tabinet">Nagano Official Tourism Website</ref>
The rest area, built in 1984, includes a two-story structure that was later converted into a multipurpose hall. The pass remains a popular destination for sightseeing and photography.
<gallery> File:Route 152 Tsuetsuki Pass restaurant.jpg|Toge no Chaya rest area File:R152-264.jpg|Signboard on National Route 152 File:杖突峠パノラマ展望台（後） - panoramio.jpg|Panoramic observation deck </gallery>
== Access ==
; Public Transport The pass is accessible by taxi from {{ill|Chino Station|en|Chino Station|WD=Q844063|lt=Chino Station|1=Chino Station}} on the {{ill|Chūō Main Line|en|Chūō Main Line|WD=Q1078110|lt=JR Chūō Main Line|1=Chūō Main Line}}, taking about 30 minutes.<ref name="tabinet" /> Seasonal bus services also connect Chino Station to the area.
; By Car From the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|en|Chūō Expressway|WD=Q1091276|lt=Chūō Expressway|1=Chūō Expressway}}, the pass is about 15 kilometers from the {{ill|Suwa Interchange|ja|諏訪インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Interchange|1=Suwa interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631823}}, with parking available near the summit.
== In Popular Culture ==
Tsuetsuki Pass appears in the manga ''{{ill|Laid-Back Camp|en|Laid-Back Camp|WD=Q28691353|lt=Laid-Back Camp|1=Laid-Back Camp}}'' (''Yuru Camp△'') by {{ill|Afro (artist)|ja|あfろ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Afro (artist)|1=Afro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q47465052}}. In chapter 16, later adapted into the anime's ninth episode, the protagonist visits the pass during a journey to the Ina Valley, capturing its scenery on her smartphone to share with a hospitalized friend.
